title: Spirit Airlines Collapses as Iran Tensions Rattle Oil Markets

blocks.content: A failed White House-backed rescue deal forced Spirit Airlines into immediate liquidation Saturday, sending shockwaves through the airline sector while geopolitical risk from President Trump's threats against Iran drove crude prices higher and kept global markets on edge.

Spirit Airlines ceased operations Saturday after a last-minute effort to arrange federal financing collapsed, marking the largest U.S. airline failure since Pan Am in 1991. The carrier's grounded fleet leaves hundreds of thousands of passengers stranded and employees facing layoffs just ahead of the holiday travel season, while competing carriers jockey to absorb its valuable airport slots and stranded travelers.

Separately, Trump signaled the United States could resume military strikes on Iran, a prospect that has crude traders bracing for potential disruption through the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil flows. Markets are pricing in a risk premium that could sustain elevated energy costs even without an immediate attack.
• S&P 500: Flat to slightly lower as airline stocks tumble while defense and energy names firm up
• FTSE 100: Edges higher supported by oil majors BP and Shell amid Middle East supply concerns
• Nasdaq: Falls as investors rotate out of consumer discretionary names toward defensive sectors

• SPIRIT AIRLINES: The budget carrier immediately grounded all flights after a $3.8 billion rescue merger with JetBlue fell apart, with the company saying it had "no choice" but to wind down and file for bankruptcy liquidation.

• FEDERAL RESERVE: The FOMC voted 11-1 to hold interest rates steady, with the lone dissent from a hawkish governor calling for higher rates, marking the most cohesive policy vote in years despite external criticism of Chair Powell.

• BERKSHIRE HATHAWAY: Warren Buffett's conglomerate posted a record $397 billion cash hoard as operating earnings doubled in the first full quarter under new CEO Greg Abel, who is sitting on an unprecedented war chest for acquisitions.

• YEN INTERVENTION: Japan's finance ministry confirmed it deployed $34.5 billion defending the yen in early trading, triggering the currency's sharpest single-session gain in years and raising the threat of further official action.

A week of heavy central bank communications concludes with Bank of England Governor Bailey testifying before Parliament, where he will face questions about the outlook for UK rates and the fiscal implications of the new government's spending plans.
